title: How to Choose a Humanoid Robot Integrator
description: What to look for in a humanoid robot integrator: task selection, real trials, safety, support and platform independence — plus the questions to ask.
quickAnswer: Choose a humanoid robot integrator on their ability to deliver outcomes, not just supply hardware. The strongest partners help you select high-fit tasks, prove them in real factory trials, integrate with proper safety set-up, and provide long-term support — and they stay platform-agnostic, matching the robot to the task rather than pushing one brand. Judge them on a measured first task and the evidence it produces, not on demos in ideal conditions.

body.text: Why the integrator matters as much as the robot

runs: A humanoid robot is only as valuable as the deployment around it. As covered in

runs.text: how to deploy a humanoid robot

runs: , success depends on task selection, trials, safe integration and support — all of which sit with your partner, not the hardware. The wrong partner can stall a project with a great robot; the right one turns it into proven ROI.

rows: They help you pick high-fit tasks, not just sell whatever you ask for.
They prove performance on your line before you commit to scaling.
They build risk assessment and safeguards in from the start.
They handle integration, training and ongoing support — not just supply.
They match the robot to the task instead of pushing one brand.
They keep uptime high and improve the system over time.

body.text: Why platform independence is a green flag

runs: An integrator tied to a single vendor has an incentive to fit your task to their robot. A

partner can choose the best robot for the job — and, just as importantly, tell you when a humanoid is not the right answer at all. That independence is a strong signal they are optimising for your outcome rather than a product sale.

body.text: A trustworthy partner will sometimes recommend a fixed robot arm, a cobot, or no robot for a given task. A provider who says a humanoid is always the answer is selling hardware, not solving your problem.
Questions to ask a prospective integrator

items: How will you help us choose the first task, and what makes a task a poor fit?
Will you run a real trial on our line, and how will success be measured?
How do you handle safety, risk assessment and staff training?
Are you tied to one platform, or do you select the best robot for the task?
What does ongoing support and improvement look like after go-live?
How do you build and prove the ROI case before we scale?

runs: A polished demonstration in ideal conditions proves little. The real test is a measured trial on your actual task, with the numbers to back it up. That is the approach HRS takes with UK manufacturers — selecting high-fit tasks, proving them in real trials, and delivering integration, safety and long-term support, while staying platform-agnostic. Start by scoping one task and letting the

faq.question: What does a humanoid robot integrator do?
faq.answer: A good integrator delivers the whole outcome, not just the robot: helping select high-fit tasks, running real trials, integrating the robot with proper safety set-up, training staff, and providing long-term support. The hardware is one part; the deployment around it is where most of the value and risk sits.

faq.question: Should an integrator be tied to one robot brand?
faq.answer: Platform independence is generally preferable. A partner tied to one vendor is incentivised to fit your task to their robot, whereas a platform-agnostic integrator can choose the best robot for the job — or advise against a humanoid when it is not the right tool.

faq.question: How do I evaluate a humanoid robot provider?
faq.answer: Judge them on outcomes and evidence: how they choose the first task, whether they run a measured trial on your line, how they handle safety and support, and whether they build a real ROI case before scaling. Be wary of decisions based only on demos in ideal conditions.
